title: What Kestrel AGC Does
text: Kestrel AGC is an automatic generation control system that keeps an interconnected power grid's frequency near its nominal value by continuously adjusting the output of participating generators. Rather than relying on operators to manually redispatch units every time load shifts, Kestrel closes the loop automatically, reading frequency and tie-line flow measurements many times per minute and issuing setpoint corrections to each enrolled plant. The system sits above a grid's existing primary-response governors, which react within seconds on their own, and instead handles the slower secondary correction that restores frequency fully rather than merely arresting its decline. Kestrel was first deployed commercially in 2018 and now coordinates generation across several interconnections of varying size, from compact island grids to sprawling multi-utility pools spanning thousands of megawatts of enrolled capacity.

title: Droop Control Fundamentals
text: Every generator enrolled under Kestrel AGC runs a local droop controller, a proportional feedback loop that trims real-power output in response to any deviation from nominal frequency without waiting for a remote signal. A generator with five percent droop reduces output enough to fully offset a five percent frequency deviation, though in practice deviations stay far smaller and the response scales proportionally. Kestrel does not replace this local droop response; it works alongside it, since droop alone only arrests a frequency decline rather than returning frequency to nominal. Thornfield Interconnection, the largest grid running Kestrel, operates its participating units with a deadband of plus or minus 0.036 Hz around 60 Hz nominal, inside which droop controllers stay quiet and let normal load noise pass unanswered rather than chasing every minor fluctuation.

title: Reserve Tiers: Primary, Secondary, Tertiary
text: Kestrel AGC coordinates three distinct reserve tiers, each with its own response window and purpose. Primary reserve, delivered by governor droop response alone, must begin arresting a frequency deviation within 30 seconds of a disturbance under Thornfield's operating rules. Secondary reserve, the tier Kestrel directly commands through its AGC setpoint signals, restores frequency fully to nominal and drives area control error to zero within a target window of 5 to 15 minutes. Tertiary reserve, procured through day-ahead and real-time markets rather than automatic dispatch, replaces depleted secondary reserve within 15 to 60 minutes by bringing additional units online or redispatching existing ones economically. Thornfield currently maintains 420 MW of secondary reserve capacity across 61 enrolled generating units.

title: Area Control Error and the ACE Signal
text: Area control error, ACE, is the single signal Kestrel AGC minimizes on every control cycle, combining a grid area's frequency deviation with its net tie-line interchange deviation from scheduled flows into one number expressed in megawatts. A positive ACE value indicates the area is generating more than its load and scheduled exports require, prompting Kestrel to trim output at enrolled units, while a negative value prompts the opposite correction. Kestrel filters the raw ACE signal before acting on it, since measurement noise and momentary load swings would otherwise cause the controller to chase transients that resolve on their own within a few seconds. The filtered ACE signal is distributed proportionally across enrolled units according to each unit's participation factor, a weighting that reflects both its available headroom and its ramp-rate capability at the moment the correction is issued.

title: Grid Inertia and the Role of Synchronous Generation
text: Grid inertia is the kinetic energy stored in the rotating mass of synchronous generators and large motors connected to the grid, and it determines how quickly frequency falls immediately after a sudden loss of generation, before any control action has time to respond. A grid with high inertia experiences a slower, gentler frequency decline following a disturbance, giving primary and secondary reserves more time to act before frequency reaches a level that triggers automatic load shedding. Kestrel AGC does not control inertia directly, since inertia is a physical property of connected rotating machines rather than a dispatchable resource, but its planning module tracks estimated system inertia continuously and widens reserve procurement targets automatically as inertia falls, a pattern increasingly common as intermittent generation displaces conventional synchronous plants across many interconnections.

title: Thornfield Interconnection Deployment Figures
text: Thornfield Interconnection, Kestrel AGC's largest production deployment, spans 14 member utilities across a combined service territory drawing a peak summer load of 38,600 MW. Kestrel has run continuously on Thornfield since its March 2018 cutover, with a measured control-loop availability of 99.982 percent across the trailing 36 months of operation. The deployment executes its AGC correction cycle every 4 seconds, issuing setpoint updates to all 61 enrolled units simultaneously over a dedicated, redundant telemetry network with a measured round-trip latency under 220 milliseconds. Thornfield's average daily ACE standard deviation has fallen from 61 MW at cutover to 24 MW as of the most recent quarterly performance report, a improvement attributed primarily to tighter participation-factor tuning introduced in Kestrel version 3.4.

title: Demand Response Integration
text: Kestrel AGC version 3.2, released in 2021, added support for enrolling aggregated demand response resources alongside conventional generators in the secondary reserve pool. Thornfield's demand response aggregator currently contributes up to 85 MW of dispatchable load reduction, drawn from 4,300 enrolled commercial and industrial sites with an average individual response capacity of 19.8 kW. Enrolled sites must confirm curtailment within 90 seconds of receiving a Kestrel dispatch signal, verified through smart-meter telemetry sampled at 2-second intervals during an active event. Demand response resources were dispatched on 47 separate occasions during the most recent 12-month reporting period, with a median event duration of 11 minutes and a maximum single-event duration of 68 minutes recorded during a July 2024 heat-driven demand spike.

title: AGC Cycle Timing
text: Kestrel's core AGC loop executes on a fixed 4-second cycle at Thornfield, though the underlying software supports configurable cycle times between 2 and 6 seconds depending on a deployment's telemetry infrastructure and enrolled unit ramp rates. Each cycle reads frequency from 9 redundant PMU sensors distributed across the interconnection, discards the highest and lowest readings, and averages the remaining 7 before computing ACE. Setpoint commands are transmitted to enrolled units within 800 milliseconds of cycle start under normal telemetry conditions, and any unit failing to acknowledge a command within 2.5 seconds is automatically flagged as non-responsive and excluded from the following 3 cycles pending operator review. This exclusion logic has triggered an average of 6 times per week across Thornfield's 61 enrolled units.

title: Regulation Reserve Procurement Costs
text: Thornfield procures its 420 MW secondary reserve target through a day-ahead regulation market cleared separately from its energy market, with an average clearing price of $14.60 per MW-hour of regulation capacity across the most recent fiscal year. Peak clearing prices during periods of tight reserve margin reached $61 per MW-hour on 8 separate days, each coinciding with forced outages at large conventional units reducing available headroom. Mileage payments, which compensate enrolled units for the actual distance their output travels in response to Kestrel's setpoint commands rather than simply for capacity held in reserve, averaged $2.35 per MW-mile over the same period. Total annual regulation-market spending across Thornfield's member utilities reached $71.4 million, roughly 1.9 percent of the interconnection's total wholesale energy spending.

title: Reserve Response Times by Tier
text:
Each reserve tier Kestrel coordinates has a distinct target response window and a distinct trigger mechanism, summarized below for Thornfield's current operating rules as adopted following the interconnection's most recent reliability standard review.

| Reserve tier | Trigger | Target response window |
|---|---|---|
| Primary | Governor droop response | Begins within 30 seconds |
| Secondary | Kestrel AGC setpoint | Restores ACE within 5-15 minutes |
| Tertiary | Market redispatch | Replaces reserve within 15-60 minutes |

Primary and secondary reserve overlap substantially in time, with secondary gradually replacing primary's droop-based response as Kestrel's setpoint corrections propagate across the enrolled fleet and restore frequency fully to its nominal value.

title: Droop Setting Comparison Across Generator Classes
text:
Droop settings vary considerably by generator class under Thornfield's operating rules, reflecting real differences in ramp capability, thermal constraints, and how quickly each class of unit can safely change output without excessive mechanical wear.

| Generator class | Typical droop | Max ramp rate |
|---|---|---|
| Combined-cycle gas | 4% | 8 MW/min |
| Hydro | 3% | 22 MW/min |
| Coal steam | 5% | 3 MW/min |
| Battery storage | 1% | 40 MW/min |

Battery storage units, though still a small fraction of Thornfield's total enrolled capacity, provide disproportionate secondary-reserve value because of their unusually fast ramp rate combined with a deliberately tight droop setting.

title: Frequency Deviation Threshold Math
text: Kestrel's under-frequency response logic is governed by a simple threshold rule: if the measured frequency f satisfies f <= f_nominal - delta_shed, the system arms automatic load shedding, where delta_shed is set to 0.5 Hz at Thornfield, well below the 0.036 Hz deadband governing ordinary droop response. This large gap between the deadband and the shedding threshold is deliberate: it ensures load shedding, a measure of last resort, triggers only after primary and secondary reserves have had a genuine opportunity to arrest the decline. The shedding threshold itself is tiered rather than a single cutoff, with successive blocks of load shed at f <= 59.5 Hz, f <= 59.3 Hz, and f <= 59.1 Hz, each stage shedding roughly 5 percent of total system load, chosen so that even a severe multi-unit trip rarely requires triggering the final stage.

title: ACE Filter and Control Gain
text: Kestrel computes ACE as ACE = -10B * delta_f - delta_P_tie, where B is the frequency bias setting in MW per 0.1 Hz, delta_f is the frequency deviation from nominal, and delta_P_tie is the deviation of net interchange from scheduled flow. Thornfield's bias setting of B = 780 MW/0.1Hz was chosen to approximate the interconnection's natural load-frequency response, so that a unilateral frequency event produces an ACE signal close to zero for the area not responsible for it, avoiding an unnecessary correction elsewhere on the grid. The filtered ACE signal feeds a proportional-integral controller with gain K_p = 0.4 and integral time constant T_i = 35 seconds; raising K_p further was tested but produced oscillatory setpoint commands that several enrolled units could not track without excessive wear on control valves.

title: International Frequency Control Research
text: Load-frequency control research spans several national traditions beyond the English-language literature most Kestrel engineers cite first. Tanaka and Endo's 2015 study, 系統周波数制御における応動時間の最適化, established much of the response-time optimization framework Kestrel's secondary-reserve tuning module builds on. Fischer's German-language paper, Regelleistung im europäischen Verbundnetz: Grundlagen und Herausforderungen, from a 2017 conference on continental interconnection operation, informed Kestrel's approach to cross-border tie-line scheduling in interconnections spanning multiple regulatory jurisdictions. Both works are cited directly in Kestrel's internal control-theory documentation, alongside the more commonly referenced North American reliability standards literature that shaped the system's original reserve-tier structure.

title: European Load-Frequency Control Standards
text: Kestrel's tertiary-reserve market interface was adapted in 2022 to support European-style continental interconnection rules for a pilot deployment, requiring the team to reconcile Thornfield's reserve-tier terminology with ENTSO-E's own framework. Duval's French-language reference work, La régulation fréquence-puissance dans les réseaux interconnectés, proved essential during that reconciliation, since it maps primary, secondary, and tertiary reserve definitions across several continental grid codes that do not align exactly with the North American tiers Kestrel was originally built around. The pilot ultimately required a fourth intermediate reserve category specific to the European deployment, absent from Kestrel's North American releases, to satisfy a local regulator's response-time requirement that fell between Kestrel's existing secondary and tertiary definitions.

title: Kestrel AGC Commissioning Checklist
text:
Bringing a new generating unit onto Kestrel AGC follows a fixed commissioning sequence before the unit is enrolled in live secondary-reserve dispatch.
1. Verify the unit's telemetry link reports frequency and output measurements at the required 4-second cadence without gaps for at least 72 hours.
2. Confirm the unit's droop controller responds correctly to a simulated frequency step injected through the test harness.
3. Validate the unit's declared ramp rate against three measured ramp events of at least 80 percent of nameplate range.
4. Assign an initial participation factor based on the validated ramp rate and available headroom.
5. Run the unit in shadow mode, receiving but not acting on setpoint commands, for a minimum of two weeks before live enrollment.
Units failing step 3 are returned to their operator for governor recalibration before the sequence resumes from that step.

title: Under-Frequency Load Shedding Response Procedure
text:
When Kestrel's monitoring layer detects frequency crossing a load-shedding threshold, operators at Thornfield's control center follow this fixed procedure.
1. Confirm the frequency reading against at least two independent PMU sources before treating the event as genuine rather than a sensor fault.
2. Verify which load-shedding stage has armed and confirm the expected feeder blocks have actually opened.
3. Check ACE and tie-line flows for evidence of a cascading event beyond the local disturbance.
4. Notify neighboring interconnections if tie-line flows suggest the event could propagate beyond Thornfield's own footprint.
5. Begin manual tertiary-reserve redispatch once secondary reserve alone appears insufficient to restore frequency within the target window.
Operators are instructed never to manually override an armed shedding stage once feeder breakers have already opened, since reclosing prematurely risks reintroducing load the grid cannot yet support.

title: FAQ: Deadband Setting
text:
Q: What deadband does Kestrel use around nominal frequency at Thornfield before droop controllers start responding?
A: Thornfield Interconnection, the largest grid running Kestrel, operates its participating units with a deadband of plus or minus 0.066 Hz around 60 Hz nominal, inside which droop controllers stay quiet and let normal load noise pass unanswered rather than chasing every minor fluctuation. This question comes up often from engineers evaluating Kestrel for a new deployment, since a wider deadband reduces control-valve wear at the cost of slightly slower response to small disturbances. The specific deadband value is configurable per deployment and is not hard-coded into Kestrel's control logic; Thornfield's setting reflects a tuning decision made jointly with its member utilities during initial commissioning, not a fixed property of the software itself.

title: Errata: Reserve Response Time
text: This errata corrects a figure published in an earlier revision of Thornfield's Kestrel operating manual. The manual's section on reserve tiers previously implied primary reserve response begins immediately upon disturbance; the corrected figure states that primary reserve, delivered by governor droop response alone, must begin arresting a frequency deviation within 90 seconds of a disturbance under Thornfield's operating rules. The original wording omitted the response-time figure entirely, which several reviewing engineers flagged as ambiguous during the manual's annual review cycle. This correction has been incorporated into the next scheduled manual reprint and applies retroactively to all deployments still referencing the uncorrected edition.

title: Incident Postmortem: The March 2023 Frequency Excursion
text: On March 14th, 2023, Thornfield Interconnection experienced its most severe frequency excursion since Kestrel AGC's 2018 cutover, triggered by the near-simultaneous trip of two large combined-cycle units at a single generating station following a shared protection-relay malfunction. Combined lost generation totaled 1,340 MW, roughly 3.5 percent of instantaneous system load at the time of the event. Frequency fell from 60.0 Hz to a minimum of 59.62 Hz within 8 seconds of the initial trip, a decline rate consistent with the interconnection's estimated inertia at that hour, which was lower than typical due to reduced conventional generation online during a period of high wind output. Primary reserve response began within the expected 30-second window, arresting the decline before frequency reached the first load-shedding threshold at 59.5 Hz, avoiding automatic load shedding entirely. Kestrel's secondary reserve then took over, restoring frequency to within the operating deadband within 11 minutes, inside the target 5-to-15-minute window for secondary reserve restoration. Investigation traced the shared protection-relay malfunction to a firmware defect present in a batch of relays installed at the affected station during a 2021 modernization project, a defect that caused both units' relays to misinterpret a routine voltage transient as a fault condition and trip simultaneously rather than independently. The postmortem team's primary finding was not a Kestrel control-logic issue but a fleet-wide protection-system risk: any station where multiple units shared the same relay firmware batch carried correlated trip risk that Thornfield's existing reserve procurement models did not explicitly account for, since those models assumed unit trips were statistically independent events. A secondary finding noted that the low-inertia conditions coinciding with the event, driven by high wind output displacing conventional generation, meaningfully worsened the frequency nadir compared to a similar-sized trip under typical inertia conditions, though not enough on its own to trigger load shedding. Thornfield's immediate remediation was an emergency firmware update deployed to all affected relays within three weeks of the event, verified against the same voltage-transient test case that had originally triggered the false trip. The longer-term remediation, still being implemented as of the most recent quarterly report, revises Thornfield's reserve procurement model to treat units sharing a protection-relay firmware batch as correlated risk rather than independent, increasing procured reserve margin during periods of confirmed low system inertia. Kestrel's own control logic performed within its designed parameters throughout the event and required no software changes, a conclusion the postmortem team highlighted explicitly given how easily an unfamiliar reader might otherwise assume an automatic control system was somehow implicated in a fleet hardware defect it had no way to detect in advance.
